coredata.dc:title: Testing and modelling of aluminium cans for prismatic lithium-ion cells under crash loading
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Cioni, Daniele
Morin, David
Strating, Arjan
Kizio, Stephan
Langseth, Magnus
Costas, Miguel
coredata.dc:description: The mechanical performance of a deep-drawn AA3003-H14 aluminium can, which serves as an external shell for vehicle lithium-ion cells, was investigated in the present study. The experimental program included material testing at different locations on the cell, at different orientations, at various strain rates, and component testing. The material was found to be mildly anisotropic and significantly strain rate sensitive. A suitable constitutive model was proposed and validated against experiments to describe the material’s mechanical behaviour. Quasi-static and dynamic tests were performed on the cans and compared with finite element simulations to validate the proposed material model. The limitations of large-scale models suitable for industrial applications were assessed. The results show how this large-scale model can be built for an accurate prediction of the crash behaviour of aluminium cans for prismatic lithium-ion cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prismatic
Lithium-ion battery
Crash
Aluminium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Numerical study of a complete cell

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from NTNU, Norway and AUDI AG, Germany. Credit for the priceless assistance at the lab must be given to Mr. Tore Wisth and Mr. Trond Auestad.
The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from NTNU and AUDI AG . Credit for the priceless assistance at the lab must be given to Mr. Tore Wisth and Mr. Trond Auestad.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Location of extraction on the can for quasi-static specimens (a) and strain rate sensitivity specimens (b). Detailed dimensions of quasi-static specimen (c) and strain rate sensitivity specimens (d), in millimetres.

Tensile test setup (a). Engineering stress–strain curve for specimens extracted from side A oriented at 0, 45 and 90 degrees with respect to the drawing direction (b). Engineering stress–strain curve for specimens extracted from sides C–D–E (c). Comparison between engineering stress–strain curve for specimens extracted from sides A–B–C–D–E, where one representative specimen per side is shown (d). For sides C–D, the specimens extracted on the lower part are chosen as representative results. All curves are plotted until failure.

UTS comparison between quasi-static and SHTB tensile tests (a). Engineering stress–strain curve for the specimens extracted from side A (b), side C (c), side D (d) and side E (e) obtained in the dynamic tensile test on the servo-hydraulic machine. Yield stress comparison between quasi-static and dynamic tensile test (f). All curves are plotted until failure.

Engineering stress–strain curve comparison between calibrated material model and dynamic tensile tests for specimens extracted from side A (a). Engineering stress–strain curve comparison between calibrated material model and quasi-static tensile tests for specimens extracted from side A (b). Failure in simulations is indicated by a triangle (shells) and a circle (solids). Experimental curves are plotted until failure.
CAD representation of the drop tower test setup: isometric view (a) and section view (b). Actual test setup for the drop tower test (c) and quasi-static test (d).
Force–displacement curves from the quasi-static (a) and dynamic component tests (b). Post-mortem components tested quasi-statically (c) and dynamically (d).

Finite element models of the component tests with element sizes of 1 mm (a) and 5 mm (b), where the colours differentiate between indenter (red), internal support (blue), and can (grey). Corner of the detailed model discretised with a single element (c) and corner of the simplified model after the modification of the mesh (d), where the colours differentiate between side A (green), side D (white), and side E (red).

Force–displacement curve of the quasi-static experiment and simulation (a). Force–displacement curves obtained from quasi-static simulations that investigated the effect of different model parameters: element size along the crack path (b), number of elements discretising the corner (c), corner shape (d), spacing between can and supports (e), effect of using the same material properties for all sides (f).
Deformed shape of the quasi-static simulation at points ①,②,③,④ (a,b,c,d).

Force–displacement curve of dynamic experiment and dynamic simulation (a). Force–displacement curve of quasi-static and dynamic simulation (b). Influence of the strain rate sensitivity on the force–displacement curve of the dynamic simulation (c). Comparison of force–displacement curves obtained by different strain rate sensitivity models in the dynamic simulation (d).
Comparison between the post-mortem deformed shape for the experiment (left) and for the simulation (right) in the quasi-static (a) and drop tower (b) test.

Simplified model used in the numerical study of a complete cell for the through-thickness compression load case (a), where the colours differentiate between indenter (red), can (grey), and jellyroll (green). Force–displacement curves resulting from the numerical study for the through-thickness compression load case (b). Asterisks indicate first failure in the can. Crack propagation for the finite element model of the through-thickness compression load case with 5 mm (c) and 1 mm (d) element sizes. Failed elements are removed from the mesh.

Simplified model used in the numerical study of a complete cell for the in-plane compression load case (a). Force–displacement curves resulting from the numerical study for the in-plane compression load case (b). Deformed shape of the detailed model for the in-plane compression load case (c). Colour plot of the damage variable on the undeformed configuration for the finite element model with 5 mm (d) and 1 mm (e) element sizes.
Engineering stress–strain curve resulting from the SHTB tests for specimens on sides A–E (a) and C–D (b). All curves are plotted until failure.
Parameters of the extended Voce work-hardening rule and the regularised CL damage model.
Parameters of the crushable foam model.

head.ce:title: Testing and modelling of aluminium cans for prismatic lithium-ion cells under crash loading

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Structural Impact Laboratory (SIMLab), Department of Structural Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU), NO-7491 Trondheim, Norway

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Structural Impact Laboratory (SIMLab), Department of Structural Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Structural Impact Laboratory (SIMLab), Department of Structural Engineering, Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU), NO-7491 Trondheim, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mechanical performance of a deep-drawn AA3003-H14 aluminium can, which serves as an external shell for vehicle lithium-ion cells, was investigated in the present study. The experimental program included material testing at different locations on the cell, at different orientations, at various strain rates, and component testing. The material was found to be mildly anisotropic and significantly strain rate sensitive. A suitable constitutive model was proposed and validated against experiments to describe the material’s mechanical behaviour. Quasi-static and dynamic tests were performed on the cans and compared with finite element simulations to validate the proposed material model. The limitations of large-scale models suitable for industrial applications were assessed. The results show how this large-scale model can be built for an accurate prediction of the crash behaviour of aluminium cans for prismatic lithium-ion cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The electric vehicle (EV) market has grown extremely fast in recent years. The most recent report from the International Energy Agency (IEA) on the EV market development reported that over 16.5 million EVs were sold in 2021, three times more than in 2018 [1]. Governments promote the sales of EVs through the use of incentives and new charging infrastructures. These policies are part of a long-term plan to decarbonise human-related activities. Thus, most car manufacturers have committed to the transition towards EVs.

Li-ion batteries have been used in consumer electronics for approximately thirty years. However, the implementation of such technology into commercial electric cars is relatively recent. Since the first adoption of EVs, an increased number of battery-related car accidents occurred, where the battery ignited after being damaged [2]. A complete understanding of the deformation mechanics of the cell is necessary to prevent such accidents.

An automotive lithium-ion battery is composed of an external metallic casing and an internal electrochemical accumulator referred to as jellyroll, see Fig. 1. The purpose of the enclosure is to prevent the evaporation of the electrolyte and protect the jellyroll from excessive deformations, which could trigger an internal short-circuit. Different cell shapes are available on the market, and the enclosure is tailored for each of them. The casing is relatively thin in pouch cells, with a thickness of approximately 0.1 mm, while it is relatively thick for cylindrical and prismatic cells, with thicknesses ranging from 0.5 to 2 mm. Prismatic cells have become the typology of choice for some major car manufacturers in recent years and are expected to spread further in the upcoming years [3].

Many authors have focused their research on characterising the mechanical properties of the jellyroll [4–14]. However, the shell casing is a fundamental component of an automotive battery cell, and it sustains a significant share of the load applied on the cell when the loading is not purely compressive, as shown in [15]. Even though some research has been performed on the shell casing of automotive cylindrical cells [15–19], very limited data has been published on the casings of prismatic cells. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, Sahraei et al. [20] published the only work partially dealing with this topic to date. Prismatic cans are commonly manufactured by deep-drawing [21], a cold stamping process aimed at obtaining high aspect-ratio components. The most common materials used for deep-drawing are non-heat-treatable aluminium alloys such as AA5xxx and AA3xxx [22,23]. Such alloys are rolled into sheets and formed into the final component [24]. The rolling process introduces anisotropy in the material [25], which is likely to be aggravated further by the deep-drawing process. Furthermore, AA3xxx alloys are reported to exhibit strain rate sensitive work-hardening [26], while negative strain rate sensitivity has been reported for AA5xxx alloys [27], adding more complexity to the mechanical behaviour of the material.

Given these materials’ complex mechanical behaviour and the deep-drawn can’s complex geometry, the problem is particularly suited to be analysed through finite element models, which are commonly used in the automotive industry as a design tool. They enable the evaluation of several design configurations, reducing the need for time-consuming and expensive testing. For instance, a vehicle’s crashworthiness is evaluated through large-scale, explicit FE simulations. In an explicit simulation, the stable time increment is governed by the size of the smallest element in the mesh. For this reason, it is unsuitable to represent thin metal sheets with solid elements for crash applications, as they would require several elements through the thickness to capture bending correctly. In these situations, shell elements are preferred for representing thin metal components. However, the correct prediction of ductile damage cannot be guaranteed when using shell elements, as it usually involves strain localisation in the form of necking, which cannot be properly described with shell elements due to the plane-stress hypothesis. For this reason, regularisation schemes are often employed with shell elements in order to alleviate mesh dependency and improve damage prediction [28–30].

In this study, an extensive experimental campaign was performed on a commercial deep-drawn AA3xxx can used in automotive prismatic batteries. The acquired data was used to calibrate a constitutive model and a failure criterion appropriate for large-scale finite element simulation of vehicles. The scientific contribution is thus linked to: (1) the assessment of the mechanical properties of the can, with a focus on the anisotropy and strain rate sensitivity and (2) the accuracy and the limitations of a shell element representation of the can when geometrical and material simplifications are applied to the model.

The investigation presented in this paper focuses on the mechanical behaviour of a deep-drawn aluminium can, which acts as the external case of a prismatic lithium-ion cell for automotive applications. The manufacturer provided the can in the disassembled state, i.e. before the jellyroll insertion and the lid assembly. The can was made of an AA3003 aluminium alloy in the cold-rolled and partially annealed condition (H14). Dimensions of the can were approximately 230 mm (length) × 100 mm (height) × 30 mm (width). For reference, each side was denoted with a different letter: A, B, C, D, and E, see Figs. 2(a) and 2(b).

The nominal wall thickness was 0.6 mm for sides A and B, 0.8 mm for sides C and D, and 1.2 mm for side E. The difference in thickness from side to side was likely due to the manufacturing process. Due to the expected mechanical heterogeneity resulting from the deep-drawing process, tensile specimens were extracted from all sides of the can. Two different flat specimen geometries were used for quasi-static and dynamic tests, as shown in Figs. 2(c) and 2(d). The specimens were cut from the can using wire electrical discharge machining (EDM) to avoid any material alteration.

The corners of the can were rounded. In particular, the corner between side E and sides A, B, C, and D had a radius of 1 mm, while the corner between side A–B and C–D had a radius of 3 mm. The influence of these small geometrical features is investigated in Section 5.2.

The tensile specimens for quasi-static tests were cut at 0, 45 and 90 degrees with respect to the drawing direction on sides A and B, as shown in figure Fig. 2(a). On the other sides, it was only possible to extract specimens oriented at 0 degrees for side C–D and at 90 degrees for side E due to limited space. The cutting patterns for sides B and D are the same as for sides A and C, respectively. Only three specimens from side B were tested to check for variations in the mechanical properties between sides A and B.

The quasi-static tests were performed on an Instron 5982 servo-hydraulic machine at 0.39 mm/min, resulting in a nominal strain rate of
5 ⋅ 1
0
− 4
s
− 1
. The force was measured with a load cell, and the in-house digital image correlation (DIC) software Ecorr [31] was used to record the strains in the specimens. For this purpose, a black-and-white speckled dot pattern was painted on the gauge of each specimen before each test. The tests were recorded at one frame per second with a Basler a2A5328-15umBAS camera with an approximate resolution of 123 pixels per millimetre. A virtual extensometer of 12.5 mm was employed in the DIC software to extract the engineering strain. Fig. 3(a) shows the setup for the quasi-static test. The engineering stress–strain curves resulting from the test are presented in Fig. 3.

The curves present a clear variation both in terms of ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and strain at failure. The variation in mechanical properties could be divided into three types: variation between different sides, between different locations on the same side, and between different orientations on the same side (anisotropy).

Anisotropy was checked for by comparing the specimens on side A, which were extracted in different orientations, see Fig. 3(b). The material exhibited a slight stress anisotropy, with the average UTS for the specimens with a 90 degrees orientation being only 2% higher than the average UTS for the specimens with a 0 degrees orientation.

Next, the variation in mechanical properties between different locations on the same side is investigated. It was found to be small for side E, relatively small for sides A–B, and significant for sides C–D, as shown in Fig. 3(c). For side E, the scatter in the UTS was just 1 MPa around a mean value of 138 MPa, while it was 17 MPa around a mean value of 174 MPa for side A and 30 MPa around a mean value of 197 MPa for side D.

Due to limited space, only one specimen was extracted and tested from the lower and upper part of sides C and D, which did not allow for a direct evaluation of the experimental scatter in the respective locations. However, no significant variation of mechanical properties between opposite sides A–B and sides C–D was found, see Fig. 3(d). Therefore, the variation between different cans is expected to be smaller than the variation between the upper and lower parts of the same side.

Nevertheless, the most remarkable difference in mechanical properties was found when comparing different sides, which is presented in Fig. 3(d). Specimens extracted from sides A, B, C and D present a lower engineering strain to fracture and an higher UTS with respect to side E. This is even more apparent in sides C and D than in sides A and B. Furthermore, it is also apparent that sides A and B exhibit the same mechanical properties.

The manufacturing process may explain the results presented in Fig. 3. During the deep-drawing process, a flat sheet of metal with constant thickness is pushed into the forming die. The material on the sides of the component is deformed and it work-hardens as a consequence, while the bottom side remains almost undeformed and its mechanical properties unchanged.

Tensile specimens for dynamic tensile tests were cut at 0 degrees with respect to the drawing direction on sides A, B, C and D, while they were cut at 90 degrees on side E due to limited space. Fig. 2(d) shows the geometry of the tensile specimen for the dynamic tensile test, while Fig. 2(b) shows the cutting pattern of the specimens. As for the quasi-static tests, the DIC software Ecorr was used for computing strains. The dynamic tensile tests were performed at different strain rates on two different machines: a Split-Hopkinson Tension Bar (SHTB), described in [32], and an Instron 5944 servo-hydraulic machine. Half of the specimens were used on the SHTB machine, and the other half on the servo-hydraulic machine. The average strain rate in the SHTB tests was 600
s
− 1
. The strain rate in the servo-hydraulic machine were
1
0
− 3
s
− 1
,
1
0
− 2
s
− 1
,
1
0
− 1
s
− 1
, and
1
s
− 1
. One single repetition for each strain rate was performed on the servo-hydraulic machine. All tests were recorded with a Phantom TMX 7510 camera. The SHTB tests were recorded at 200000 frames per second with an approximate resolution of 53 pixels per millimetre. The dynamic tensile tests performed with the servo-hydraulic machine were recorded with an acquisition frequency between 1–10000 frames per second and resolutions between 110–150 pixels per millimetre, depending on the strain rate. A virtual extensometer of 4.5 mm was employed in the DIC software to extract the engineering strain. The stress in the SHTB test was obtained from classical wave propagation theory.

The stress–strain curves from the SHTB tests presented oscillations in the first part, which were attributed to the imbalance in the force between the input and the output bar. A comparison between the input and output bar strain signals confirmed that the equilibrium requirement was not fulfilled in the first part of the test. A possible explanation for this behaviour is the high impedance that the stress wave encountered at the interface between the input bar and the thin, rectangular specimens. Therefore, the first part of the stress–strain curve obtained from the SHTB test was discarded, and only the UTS values were used for validation purposes. Fig. 4(a) shows a comparison between the UTS of the specimens in quasi-static tests versus dynamic tests performed on the SHTB. Results from sides C and D are plotted together under the label “side D”. It is apparent that the material exhibits a significant strain rate sensitivity, even considering the scatter in the experimental data. Here, it is important to point out that the specimens tested on the SHTB from sides C and D were extracted from the upper part of the respective side. Hence, they are expected to exhibit a higher UTS compared to the specimen extracted from the lower part, as discussed in Section 2.2.

Next, the results of the tests on the servo-hydraulic machine are presented. Figs. 4(b)–4(e) show the engineering stress–strain curves of the specimens tested at intermediate values of strain rate in the servo-hydraulic machine. As opposed to the SHTB tests, the specimens from sides C and D tested on the servo-hydraulic machine were extracted from the lower part of the respective side, except for one specimen on side D, which was extracted at the intermediate level. This is readily apparent in Fig. 4(d), where the specimen extracted at the intermediate level is the one that was tested at a strain rate of
1
0
− 3
s
− 1
.

In general, the curves show a distinctive feature: for increasing values of strain rate, the yield stress decreases and the work-hardening increases. However, a comparison between the yield stress obtained in these tests and the quasi-static tests, shown in Fig. 4(f), indicates that the variation does not exceed the experimental scatter of the quasi-static tests. Therefore, it is not reasonable to conclude that the decrease in yield stress due to an increase in strain rate is an actual feature of the investigated material. On the other hand, the observed increase in work-hardening is attributed to strain rate sensitive work-hardening, as it is typical in most AA3xxx alloys [26,33].
In the following, a material model suitable for describing in a finite element model the material properties observed in Section 2 is presented.

Given its low anisotropy, the material is modelled with an isotropic, hypoelastic–plastic material model with associated plasticity, von Mises yield surface and isotropic work-hardening. The yield function reads:
(1)
f =
σ
eq
−
σ
y
≤ 0 ,
where
σ
eq
is the Von Mises equivalent stress and
σ
y
is the flow stress. The flow stress is assumed to be a function of the equivalent plastic strain p and equivalent plastic strain rate
p
̇
:
(2)
σ
y
( p ,
p
̇
)
=
σ
0
+ R
( p ,
p
̇
)
,
where
σ
0
is the initial yield stress, and R is the isotropic work-hardening.

The isotropic work-hardening was described with Voce’s law, viz
(3)
R =
∑
i = 1
2
Q
i
1 − exp
−
θ
i
Q
i
p
,
where
θ
1 , 2
are the initial work-hardening moduli and
Q
1 , 2
are the saturation values of the work-hardening. The value of
Q
2
was assumed to be dependent on the strain rate following the work of [26], while
θ
1 , 2
and
Q
1
were not affected by the strain rate:
(4)
Q
2

Q
2

p

̇

,
where
Q
2

is the quasi-static value of
Q
2
,
C
q
is the strain rate sensitivity exponent, and
p
̇
0
is the reference equivalent plastic strain rate, taken equal to the nominal strain rate of the slowest test (
1
0
− 3
s
− 1
).

The damage was represented using the Cockcroft–Latham (CL) fracture criterion [34] with the addition of a regularisation scheme proposed by Costas et al. [30]. The CL criterion defines the damage variable D as
(5)
D = ∫
1
W
C
σ
I
d p ,
where
W
C
is the damage parameter obtained from material tests,
σ
I
is the major principal stress,
σ
I
= max
σ
I
, 0
, and p is the equivalent plastic strain. Failure occurs at an integration point when the damage variable D reaches a value of 1, and elements are removed from the mesh when their middle through-thickness integration point fails. Furthermore, the regularisation scheme reduces the ductility of shell elements subjected to membrane loading to account for local necking. The element ductility is balanced by accounting for the relative amount of bending and membrane loading,
(6)
W
C
= Ω
W
C
b
+
( 1 − Ω )
W
C
m
,
where the weighting parameter Ω defines the membrane-to-bending load ratio based on the through-thickness strain,
(7)
Ω =
1
2
|
ɛ
p , 33
T
−
ɛ
p , 33
B
|
max
|

T
|
,
|

p , 33
B
|
,
where
ɛ
p , 33
T
and
ɛ
p , 33
B
are the through-thickness plastic strains at the top and bottom integration points of the element, respectively. In Eq. (6), the parameter
W
C
m
depends on the element’s aspect ratio
l
e
/
t
e
by the expression
(8)
W
C
m
=
W
C
1
+
W
C
s

l

e
− 1
.
The parameters
W
C
b
,
W
C
l
,
W
C
s
, and c were obtained by inverse modelling of the tensile test. The reader is referred to [30] for more information about the regularisation model.
The material model previously described was implemented as an user-hardening (UHARD/VUHARD) combined with an user-defined-field (USDFLD/VUSDFLD) in Abaqus/Standard and Abaqus/Explicit 2019 [35].

The material properties were considered constant on each individual side of the can. This assumption was supported by experimental evidence on sides A, B and E. At the same time, it did introduce a certain degree of approximation for sides C and D, where a significant variation of mechanical properties was observed. However, each side was modelled with a different set of parameters. Therefore, three material cards were calibrated, corresponding to sides A or B, C or D, and E. This simplification was supported by the experiments, where no significant difference in mechanical properties was reported between opposite sides of the can. As for the corners, the material card of side E was used to model the 1 mm radius between side E and sides A, B, C, and D, while the material card of side D was used to model the 3 mm radius between sides A–B and C–D, see Fig. 8(c).

The dynamic tensile tests performed on the servo-hydraulic machine were used to calibrate the quasi-static material properties and the strain rate sensitivity. A single representative engineering stress–strain curve was used for the calibration. The parameter identification, performed with the software LS-OPT [36], was divided into two steps:

ce:list-item.ce:para: An inverse model of the tensile test performed with a fine mesh of solid elements in Abaqus/Standard 2019 [35] was used to identify the work-hardening parameters. The specimen was modelled with 10 elements through the thickness.
The work-hardening parameters obtained in the previous step and the DIC images of the experiments were used to calibrate the damage model.

ce:section.ce:para: The stress–strain curves resulting from the inverse modelling for side A are shown in Fig. 5(a), while the parameters of the extended Voce work-hardening rule and the damage model obtained from the calibration are given in Table 1. The stress–strain curves resulting from the inverse modelling for sides D and E had a similar fit with the experimental results as for side A and were omitted for brevity.

It is important to notice that the material model requires to be calibrated on a solid-element model due to the adopted damage regularisation scheme, but it is intended to be used with shell elements. For this reason, the results from an inverse model of the tensile test performed with shell elements were compared to the results from the solid-element model, see Fig. 5(a). The results from the shell element simulations agreed well with the solid element simulations until necking, after which the two curves began to spread apart. This discrepancy is attributed to the shell elements not being able to represent necking correctly.

A shortcoming of the adopted damage criterion is the lack of an explicit dependence on the strain-rate. This is apparent in Fig. 5(a), where the predicted engineering failure strain is just slightly lower for the dynamic tensile test simulation performed at a nominal strain rate of
1
s
− 1
than for the one performed at a nominal strain rate of
1
0
− 3
s
− 1
. This small reduction is due to the strain-rate sensitive work-hardening, which increased the stress and leaded to a slightly higher damage accumulation rate throughout the simulation. However, the experimental results showed an increase of the engineering strain to failure with an increase of the strain-rate, which could be captured by making the CL damage criterion strain-rate dependent. Since the model is intended for industrial applications, this modification has not been included in order to reduce the complexity and keep the prediction conservative.

In order to validate the calibrated material parameters, a tensile test simulation of the quasi-static specimen shown in Fig. 2 was performed, and the stress–strain curves of the simulation were compared with the experimental ones for side A, see Fig. 5(b). The simulation was able to replicate the experimental results, even though the stress–strain curve laid on the lower bound of the experimental scatter. A similar result was obtained for sides D and E.

An evaluation of the performance of the strain rate sensitivity of the model was done by extrapolating the UTS predicted by the model to a strain rate of
600
s
− 1
and comparing the prediction with the results from the SHTB tests. The model prediction, shown in Fig. 4(a), laid on the upper bound of the experimental scatter for sides A and E, while it was underestimated for side D. It is important to point out that the calibration on side D was performed on the specimen with the lowest mechanical properties, while the SHTB results for side D were obtained from the specimens with the highest mechanical properties since they were extracted closer to the top of the can. Therefore, this discrepancy was to be expected. Overall, the correlation between the extrapolated and experimental values was satisfactory.

The proposed constitutive and failure model was validated against quasi-static and dynamic component tests. The experimental setup was specifically designed to assess the performance of the material model in correctly representing plasticity and fracture under different loading rates. Nevertheless, it is important to point out that these tests are not necessarily representative of real crash loadings. The can was laid upside-down on two L-shaped steel supports specifically designed for the purpose, as seen in Fig. 6(d). The supports were designed to have a 2 mm gap with respect to the internal surface of the can walls to avoid damaging the can during the setup. This gap, together with the dimensions of the components, can be seen in Fig. 6(b). The impactor used in the test had a hemispherical shape with a radius of 75 mm. The geometry of the can posed a significant challenge to the repeatability of the test because the high slenderness of the can lateral walls promoted buckling under relatively small compressive loads. Another challenge was promoting failure in the can to validate the damage model. To that end, two circular slits were wire-cut to promote a crack which would propagate through side A/B, as shown in Fig. 6(b).

The quasi-static tests were performed on an Instron 5566 servo-hydraulic machine at a cross-head speed of 5 mm/min. The can was laid upside-down on the two steel supports, which were screwed to a steel platform. The force was recorded by a load cell, while the displacement of the impactor was computed using DIC. The displacement of the supports was also tracked to confirm that they were fixed during the test. Three repetitions of the test were performed. Fig. 6(d) shows the test setup. One limitation of the chosen setup is the uncertainty in the distance between the internal surface of the can and the supports, whose effect is analysed in Section 5.

Fig. 7(a) shows the force–displacement curves resulting from the quasi-static tests, where it can also be seen that the repeatability of the tests was satisfactory. The first two specimens presented a very similar response curve during the first 50 mm of displacement, while some differences appeared afterwards. However, the third specimen underwent a non-symmetric buckling mode, in contrast with the symmetric buckling mode of the first two specimens. A comparison between the deformed shapes is presented in Fig. 7(c). The different buckling mode of the third test is also evident in the force–displacement curve, which presents a lower force throughout the test.

During the tests, the cans folded and cracked as a consequence of the imposed deformation. The deformation process is better explained by looking at the deformed shape at different values of displacement, as shown in Fig. 10. After a short initial loading phase, which corresponded to an almost linear response on the force displacement curve shown in Fig. 9(a), the cans began to fold extensively ①. Afterwards, the cans kept folding while the gap between the internal support and the cans was reducing ②. After the gap had been closed, the force raised quickly and the damage started accumulating in the slits until the onset of fracture initiation ③. The decrease in the force after damage initiation was due to the fracture propagating through sides A–B ④. Subsequent increases in the force represent a temporary interruption of the fracture propagation.

The dynamic component tests were performed on an Instron CEAST 9530 drop tower with an impact velocity of 4.5 m/s. The same test setup as that of the quasi-static tests was used, and three repetitions of the test were performed. The test setup is shown in Figs. 6(a) and 6(b).

Fig. 7(b) shows the force–displacement curves resulting from the tests, with good repeatability. All three tests presented very similar response curves, and all three specimens underwent a symmetric buckling mode. The average energy absorption was 142 J for the quasi-static tests and 206 J for the drop tower tests.

Finite element models of both the quasi-static and dynamic component tests were built in Abaqus/Explicit 2019. The models took advantage of the symmetry of the problem and consisted of one-half of the can. It is important to note that using a quarter-symmetric model would inhibit the non-symmetric buckling modes observed in the experiments. The material model described in Section 3.1 was adopted. The hemispherical indenter and the supports were modelled as rigid surfaces. The simplification of using rigid bodies instead of deformable bodies was assessed during a preliminary parametric study, where it was found to have no influence on the final result. The can was modelled with 33590 four-node shell elements with reduced integration and five through-thickness integration points, with an average element size of 1 mm and a minimum element size of 0.5 mm. A mesh sensitivity study confirmed that using element sizes smaller than 1 mm did not affect significantly the simulation results. Hourglass control was included to prevent spurious deformation modes. The general contact algorithm with manual contact surface definition was used, and the global friction coefficient was set to 0.3. The friction influence was investigated in a preliminary study, and it was found to have a negligible influence on the results. Semi-automatic mass scaling with a target time increment of
1 . 4 ⋅ 1
0
− 4
s
was used in the quasi-static simulations, and the kinetic energy was checked to ensure quasi-static conditions. The setup and mesh of the model are shown in Fig. 8(a).

Additionally, a simplified model was created to assess the effect of the geometric features on the results. The simplified model had straight corners instead of round corners, which allowed for a coarser mesh size to be used. Simplified representations with both 1 mm and 5 mm element sizes were assessed.

The force–displacement curves of the quasi-static simulation are compared against the experimental ones in Fig. 9(a). Quantitatively, the detailed model underestimated the initial force peak by 4%, while the energy absorption predicted by the simulation was 10% higher than the experimental value. Furthermore, failure was correctly predicted at 23 mm of displacement, but the force peak at failure was overestimated by 24%.

The folding/cracking response was captured by the detailed model, as shown in Fig. 12. Even though the crack propagated longer in the simulation than in the experiments, the number of folds in the simulation was consistent with the experiments.

At this point, a sensitivity study was conducted to investigate the influence of some experimental and modelling parameters. First, the influence of the gap between the internal surface and the supports on the mechanical response of the can was analysed, see Fig. 9(e). Increasing the gap had the effect of delaying the rise in force prior to the first failure point, and the consequence was an offset between the curves and a drift in the response in the last part of the curve.

Secondly, in order to use such a model in large-scale explicit simulations, the element size needs to be increased. For this reason, the performance of the simplified model was assessed, see Fig. 9(d). The simplified model was not able to reproduce the experimental force–displacement curve accurately, and refining the mesh size did not improve the result significantly, meaning that the geometric simplification of using straight corners has the strongest influence on correctly describing the mechanical response of the can. With the aim of investigating further the effect of the corner shape, a simulation was run on the detailed model with one element inclined at 45 degrees instead of two elements for discretising the round corner between sides A/B/C/D and E, see Fig. 8(c). The results, shown in Fig. 9(c), are almost indistinguishable, suggesting that a modification of the mesh in the corner could improve the results. This idea was investigated in the simplified representation by moving the nodes of side E in order to achieve an inclination of 15 degrees of the row of elements on side A that constitute the corner, see Fig. 8(d). This modification reduced the first peak of force from 3.9 kN to 3.6 kN, as shown in Fig. 9(d). Even though the modification slightly improved the prediction, it is clear that it alone is insufficient for compensating the geometry simplification.

Another requirement for the model to be suitable for large-scale explicit simulations is that the damage criterion must be able to perform adequately for different element sizes. For this purpose, a local mesh sensitivity study was performed, where the element size was increased to 2 mm in the crack propagation region and left unchanged at 1 mm elsewhere. The resulting force–displacement curves, shown in Fig. 9(b), are indistinguishable until the failure point, which took place at the same cross-head displacement for the coarser mesh. The crack propagation after the first peak was slower for the coarser mesh, while the rest of the curve is comparable between the two simulations. In addition, it is interesting to see that in Fig. 9(d) the crack initiation took place at the same displacement value for the 1 mm and 5 mm mesh size, indicating that the mesh regularisation scheme worked adequately.

As detailed in Section 2, the material heterogeneity resulting from the deep-drawing process requires extensive testing to be characterised. Such an intensive experimental campaign is costly and time-consuming, with the consequence of being difficult in an industrial context. For this reason, the effect of using the same material for all the sides of the can was evaluated, see Fig. 9(f). Adopting the material properties of sides A and D for the entire can yielded a response which was very similar to the baseline simulation. On the other hand, assigning the material properties of side E to the entire can did influence significantly the results in the first part of the curve. It is important to point out that side E corresponds most-likely to the raw material, and its mechanical properties were the most different compared to the other sides. Therefore, if the can is to be modelled with only one material card, it seems reasonable to use material data from one of the vertical sides of the can, which undergoes most of the plastic deformation caused by the deep-drawing process, instead of using the material data from the bottom of the can, which undergoes minor plastic deformation.

In general, a dynamic response may differ from a quasi-static one due to inertia effects and material strain rate sensitivity. Fig. 11(a) compares the results of the dynamic simulation with the experiment results from the drop tower tests. Again, the detailed model underestimated the initial force peak by 4%. The displacement at first failure was underestimated by 8%, while the force peak at first failure was overestimated by 8%. The energy absorption predicted by the simulation was 6% lower than the experimental value.

As for the quasi-static model, the deformed shape of the component resulting from the drop tower tests was well captured by the model, as shown in Fig. 12. The curvature of side E was more pronounced in the simulation than in the experiment, while the folding pattern agreed with the experiment.

In order to check for inertia effects, the dynamic simulation was run with the strain rate sensitivity parameter
C
q
set to zero. The difference between the quasi-static response and the dynamic response without rate-sensitivity was negligible, see Fig. 11(c), indicating that the strain rate sensitivity is the most important factor differentiating the quasi-static response from the dynamic response in the simulations.

The strain rate sensitive work-hardening model presented in Section 3.1 is not readily available in most commercial finite element codes. For this reason, a simpler viscoplasticity model’s performance was compared with the implemented model’s performance. The viscoplasticity model chosen for this comparison was a modified formulation of the Johnson–Cook model [37], which is readily available in most commercial softwares:
(9)
σ
y
( p ,
p
̇
)
=
(
σ
0
+ R )
1 +
p
̇
p
̇
0
C
,
where C is the strain rate sensitivity parameter. The same parameters shown in Table 1 were used, except for C , which was recalibrated following the same procedure as in Section 3.2, resulting in values of 0.007, 0.010 and 0.015 for side A, D and E, respectively. It is important to point out that obtaining a good fit of the experimental data with this model was not possible. As expected, the model overestimated the yield stress since, in Eq. (9), the parameter
σ
0
is also affected by the strain rate sensitivity. Furthermore, the JC model underestimated the stress in the second part of the stress–strain curve. Fig. 11(d) shows the comparison between the force–displacement curves of the simulation of the component test obtained with the strain rate sensitive work-hardening model and the modified JC model. It is interesting to notice that the prediction given by the JC model agreed reasonably well with the one given by the implemented model, even though the fit to the tensile test results was poorer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Numerical study of a complete cell
ce:section.ce:para: Up to this point, the can was considered as a standalone component, and its mechanical performance was evaluated as such. However, in a car crash scenario, the can would enclose a jellyroll, which would take a share of the load acting on the cell. The contribution of the enclosure to the overall mechanical performance of the cell has been investigated for cylindrical cells by Wierzbicki and Sahraei [16], Sahraei et al. [17], Zhang and Wierzbicki [15], Zhu et al. [18]. The authors showed that the enclosure strength has a limited influence on the overall mechanical performance of the cell when compressive loads are dominant since the jellyroll is likely to take the most significant share of the load. For such scenarios, a simplified representation of the can could yield a good approximation of the behaviour of the can.

In the following, the accuracy of a simplified representation of the can with straight corners and an element size of 5 mm is assessed for a more realistic scenario in which the can encloses a jellyroll. In this numerical study, the detailed model of the can was taken as a benchmark for evaluating the performance of the simplified model. Two loading configuration were investigated: through-thickness compression, see Fig. 13(a), and in-plane compression, see Fig. 14(a). Preliminary simulations showed that, for the in-plane loading configuration, the can would deform with no significant damage until an very high value of intrusion. For this reason, the in-plane loading scenario was modelled with two cells side by side, mimicking the disposition of a car battery module.

Only a quarter of the model was represented in both loading configuration by imposing symmetric boundary conditions. This simplification was possible by assuming that the can is closed on the open side by a lid that has perfect continuity with the other sides (no local weakening due to welding) and has the same properties and thickness as side E. The indenter was the same as in Section 5.1, while the platform supporting the case was represented by a rigid surface. The speed of the indenter was 5 mm/min, the same as in the quasi-static component tests. The same choices as in Section 5 regarding contact, friction, hourglass control and mass scaling were taken.

The complex layered structure of the jellyroll was lumped into a single part, a process often referred to as homogenisation. For a review of homogenised models, the reader is referred to [38]. The jellyroll was modelled with 6840 eight-node solid elements with reduced integration, resulting in an average element size of 3 mm.

The material model chosen for the jellyroll was the isotropic crushable foam model available in Abaqus, based on the Deshpande–Fleck constitutive model [39]. The elastic and plastic properties were based on [40], with a plastic stress–strain curve in the form
A
ɛ
n
. The compression yield stress ratio
k
c
was set to 1.73 in order to retain associated plastic flow. Damage and strain rate sensitivity were not included in the jellyroll material model (see Table 2).

The force–displacement curves of the detailed and simplified model for the through-thickness compression simulations are compared in Fig. 13(b). An asterisk was added to indicate first element failure in the can. For this loading scenario, the simplified model yielded the same results as the detailed model in terms of force, while it underestimated by 8% the displacement at failure. On the other hand, for the in-plane loading scenario, the simplified model yielded a consistently higher force than the detailed model, up to 11% more at the end of the simulation. This loading scenario did not induce complete failure in any element for neither of the models, but the can folded multiple times and many elements presented a value of damage close to the element deletion threshold.

Figs. 13(c) and 13(d) show the deformed shape for the through-thickness loading simulation, with colour maps of the damage variable D . Here, the simplified model predicted the same fracture location and length as in the detailed model, even though the crack initiated later in the former. Nevertheless, a similar comparison of the deformed shape is not feasible for the in-plane loading scenario since the folding would hinder the visualisation of the results. For this reason, the undeformed configuration is shown instead, see Figs. 14(d) and 14(e). The damage location and intensity, closely related to the folding pattern, were reasonably predicted by the simplified model. Yet, the simplified model could not capture some damaged areas due to the element size, which influenced the folding pattern.

On the experimental side, the quasi-static tests showed a significant variation in mechanical properties, which has not been reported in the available literature on automotive prismatic battery cans [20]. While this effect may be relevant for certain applications, the simulations showed that the use of one single set of material properties for the entire can still delivers accurate results.

As of today, the authors are not aware of any literature on strain rate sensitivity of deep-drawn AA3xxx aluminium cans. The current study suggests that the strain rate sensitive work-hardening is a relevant factor in the dynamic response of the material. Indeed, the simulations in this study showed that the strain rate sensitivity was the most relevant parameter differentiating the quasi-static and dynamic responses. It is noteworthy that a customary viscoplastic formulation of the strain rate sensitivity performed significantly well compared to the proposed model, even though it was not possible to obtain a good fit to the tensile experimental data during the calibration.

On the numerical side, the presented finite element model yielded adequate results compared to the experiments. A sensitivity study of the finite element model’s parameters was reported in Sections 5.2 and 5.3. For the particular test setup investigated in this study, the gap between the can and the support markedly influences the response, resulting in the most significant uncertainty in comparing the model and the experiments.

Furthermore, the simulation results showed that the rounded corners of the can had a considerable influence on the force–displacement curve, much stronger than the element size. Such a small geometrical feature is unfeasible in large scale models, meaning that it may be a limiting factor for the accurate prediction of the buckling response. It could be reasonable to think that a modification of the mesh in the corner area, such as tapering the otherwise straight corner, could effectively improve the results of the simplified model. However, the simulations showed that, while the first peak in force is slightly reduced, the overall prediction is not improved.

The capabilities of the simplified model were tested for a more realistic scenario in Section 5.4 by inserting a jellyroll inside the can. Here, the simplified model proved to be effective for through-thickness load cases, where the folding mechanism does not play an important role. On the other hand, it could not capture the folding pattern of the in-plane load case due to the excessive element size, leading to a discrepancy in the force and damage prediction.

Overall, the simplified model could predict cracks accurately, which is important for the design of safe and durable EV battery modules. In fact, even though the stiffness of the cell comes mainly from the jellyroll, the structural integrity of the can is necessary to prevent electrolyte leakage, which would dry up the jellyroll and lead to cell malfunctioning. Furthermore, the exposure of the jellyroll to sharp surfaces might trigger a thermal runaway due to local perforation of the separator.

This investigation presented an experimental and numerical study on the mechanical properties of an aluminium enclosure for the electrochemical accumulator of automotive lithium-ion batteries. The material properties were investigated in a material testing campaign, in which tensile specimens were extracted and tested at different locations on the cell, at different orientations, and at various strain rates. The variation in mechanical properties, which was found to be significant when comparing different sides of the can, is attributed to the manufacturing process. Moreover, the material exhibited positive strain rate sensitive work-hardening.

The material’s mechanical behaviour was described by a phenomenological constitutive model and a regularised failure criterion, which were validated against experiments. Finite element models of the can were compared with quasi-static and dynamic tests to validate the proposed material model. Furthermore, a numerical study in which a jellyroll was present inside the can was conducted. Both fine and coarse shell element sizes were assessed. The proposed material model appropriately predicted the mechanical response of the can for the simulations with a fine element size. However, the coarse element size prevented the finite element model from correctly capturing the deformation mechanism and the mechanical response when the can underwent extensive folding.

The numerical study showed that a coarse shell element size model, paired with a regularised failure criterion, could deliver results comparable to a fine element size model in a through-thickness loading scenario. At the same time, the force and damage prediction became less accurate for in-plane loading scenarios, where the excessive element size prevented the model to capture the folding pattern correctly. Overall, such a model is able to predict cracks accurately, which is fundamental for the design of safe and durable EV cells.

Daniele Cioni: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. David Morin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Arjan Strating: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Stephan Kizio: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Conceptualization. Magnus Langseth: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Miguel Costas: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support from NTNU, Norway and AUDI AG, Germany . Credit for the priceless assistance at the lab must be given to Mr. Tore Wisth and Mr. Trond Auestad.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: The original engineering stress–strain curves obtained in the SHTB tests are included in the following (see Fig. A.1). As explained in the text, the stress–strain curves from the SHTB tests presented oscillations in the first part, which were attributed to the imbalance in the force between the input and the output bar. A comparison between the input and output bar strain signals confirmed that the equilibrium requirement was not fulfilled in the first part of the test. A possible explanation for this behaviour is the high impedance that the stress wave encountered at the interface between the input bar and the thin, rectangular specimens. Therefore, only the UTS values were used for validation purposes.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of a split-Hopkinson tension bar in a mutual assessment of experimental tests and numerical predictions
Int J Impact Eng

J Mech Phys Solids

sb:publisher.sb:name: Journal of the Institute of Metals

Eng Fract Mech
